new_rules.rule: Any machine-generated governed artifact must be validated against its canonical schema at the boundary before it can drive workflow state or update shared machine-facing artifacts.
applies_to: boundary validators for learning outputs, compliance maps, fix-items maps, and rulebook/evidence writers
new_rules.do: Before writing `learning.json`, updating `rulebook.json`, or emitting repair evidence, run the raw payload through the declared schema such as `LearningOutput.safeParse(...)` or the shared compliance/fix-item schema; if validation fails, stop the update and return a closed validation error.
new_rules.dont: Parse model JSON and append `new_rules`, `compliance_map`, or `fix_items` directly into governed artifacts just because the fields look present, then discover later that malformed entries corrupted machine-facing state.
new_rules.source: Round 7: reviewer-codex:group-3 learning and repair outputs were allowed to update governed artifacts without boundary schema validation.

existing_rules_covering.explanation: This is the same general failure pattern COD-003 already covers: severity metadata must be preserved end-to-end without lossy remapping. Flattening self-check warnings into material errors is a severity rewrite that contaminates downstream repair and result logic.

COD-009 already requires one canonical result/evidence model for terminal artifacts, postmortems, and KPI summaries on every exit path. The missing canonical run-postmortem builder for zero-round, blocked, and normal exits is exactly that violation.

This is the concrete manifestation of the same severity-preservation problem: `toValidationIssue` hardcodes `severity: 'error'`, which is a lossy remap of canonical self-check severity and therefore already falls under COD-003.

IMP-006 already covers dead code and stale leftovers after rewrites. A redundant `pathExists` check on a just-written file and an unused `mdLower` variable are straightforward dead-code/cleanup misses, not a new rule category.
